review_text: Finally caught this in the theatre. It was amazing, and is an entirely different experience from watching at home.

Good acting, great direction, and lifelong phobias of what's lurking beneath the surface and out of sight. I don't like the pacing, and the movie feels like it can't decide if its horror, thriller, or action, but it could work as either if it would just stick with one, that's the only aspect in which Jaws 2 surpasses this classic. Innovative direction despite a troubled production make this movie work for the better, the film does a really good job portraying money hungry government who turn blind eyes to a problem. The jumpscares keep the audience invested and the beach attacks pull the audience in for the rest of the movie. Very few flaws that are incredibly easy to overlook due to everything that the film does right. Summer is not Summer with Jaws. Jaws and Jaws 2 are the first 2 movies of any Summer someone wants to spend in any body of water.
This movie is the most amazing shark movie I ever watch

I saw this movie when I was only 10 years old. As a kid, I was always fascinated with sharks and my dad knew this and took me and my older sister to see it. It was rated PG but if this movie was made today it would definitely be R rated but not as good imo. Not even close. That being said, I loved this movie and sat and watched it in awe in the theater and have seen it at least 15x since, maybe more. The movie came out in the ‘70s, but still resonates with the masses today bc it is wonderfully scary and was done by Spielberg. Don’t bother with any of the sequels. This is the only one you need to watch. The acting is outstanding and it is not a corny killer shark movie by any means. It is riveting, scary and highly entertaining. Some parts of the movie are quite bloody, but in a very good way strangely enough. Scheider, Shaw and Dreyfus are all amazing and this cult classic will have you on the edge of your seat until the very end. One of my favorite movies of all time!

Without Spielberg’s precise direction and John Williams’ iconic score, this film wouldn’t be the same. Together, they engineered suspense through restraint, atmosphere, and pure cinematic control — redefining the modern blockbuster in the process.
Quite possibly the greatest horror movie of all time. My personal favorite. Just spectacular.

This is truly a game changer in history with top tier performances, direction, editing, production and the shark for most of the film looked really good. The only flaws in this amazing film is the short section of live shark footage and IMO the mechanical shark was shown just a little bit too much in the final 10 or so minutes, but this is still and epic intense and thrilling experience.
This movie is pure art!

Considered one of the greatest films of all time. A testament I certainly agree with. The acting, perfect. The music, a blessing to all ears. The cinematography, excellent. The thrill, flawless. No matter what angle you look at this movie it is flawless. Scheider, Dreyfuss, and Shaw deliver the best performances of their respective careers. A lot of modern critics pan this movie for how the effects have aged but in my opinion they hold up great, even when the shark finally appears. The story is written so well and this movie will forever stand the test of time.
Watched this over Christmas for maybe the 100th time. Still the best movie ever made in my opinion.

The filmmaking has such a clear high quality & skill that holds up 50 years later as a staple blockbuster. The atmosphere of suspenseful anticipation is almost constant, emphasize by Williams’ timeless score. There are well written characters who have conflicts & motives that are worth exploring. There is a noticeable pacing dip in the second act but everything else moves lightning quick.

I was scared stiff back in 1975, so why did I go to the cinema to see this on the big screen again to mark its 50th anniversary? Why? Because I remember how much a good fright and scare is for the soul. It still makes me scared to death of sharks. Living in Australia where shark attacks seem more common of late does not encourage a trip to the ocean for a swim by this filmgoer!
I’ve watched this film dozens of times, in different formats and settings, and it never disappoints. It’s been my favorite for decades, and it’ll probably never swim away from that top spot.

Let’s get one thing straight: Jaws is the reason an entire generation refuses to swim in any body of water larger than a bathtub. And honestly? Valid. The movie starts with a cheerful beach scene—sunshine, warm sand, happy people—and then immediately says, “Actually, let’s ruin that forever.” A shark the size of a Ford F-150 rolls in like it owns the place, and suddenly everyone’s summer vacation turns into a don’t-be-the-snack competition. The Cast: Chief Brody: A man who hates water but took a job on an island. Bold choice. He spends the entire movie looking like someone who just remembered he left the oven on. Quint: A fisherman who's about 40% sea salt, 40% trauma, and 20% beer. Gives the greatest monologue of all time and is also the human equivalent of a rusty anchor. Hooper: A marine biologist who shows up with fancy gadgets and “science” and somehow still ends up inside a shark cage questioning all of his life choices. Plot in Short: A shark starts eating the residents of Amity Island one by one, and instead of closing the beach, the mayor basically says, “But… tourism dollars.” Truly the most accurate portrayal of local government ever put on film. Special Effects: Bruce the mechanical shark looks like a rubber bath toy that hit the gym, but when he pops out of the water with those dead eyes? Terrifying. Peak nightmare fuel. The Music: John Williams wrote two notes—literally two—and managed to permanently raise everyone’s heart rate whenever they hear duunnn dun… duuuun dun… even if they’re just dipping their toes into a hotel pool. Final Thoughts: Jaws is a classic masterpiece that teaches us important lessons, like: Don’t swim at night. Don’t trust politicians in pastel suits. And if a guy offers to kill a shark for $10,000 while chugging beer and laughing like a pirate… maybe just let him? 10/10. Would watch again, but only on land.

If you like faulty animatronic sharks, dolly zooms & beaches you’re gonna love this. But who hasn’t already seen jaws (freaks)?! I used to get worried there was a shark in the toilet after watching it when I was 10, and still check now before I sit on the loo (and I’m 40). Love the music, acting and directing - "Smile you son of a….". Proper good cinema from the Hitchcock playbook (without the obsession with birds). The book isn’t as bad as people say either. Play it backwards and sharks give birth to humans.

So... the thing about this movie is that... yes, I liked it growing up. I've seen all four (my childhood favorite was Jaws the Revenge). However, you could call this movie evil for what it did to all the sharks. Growing up, I wanted to be a marine biologist, and sharks were essentially my spirit animal. When I heard that this movie caused a shark panic, and people started killing sharks FOR NO REASON just because this movie portrayed them as merciless killers, I was obviously heartbroken. I don't really feel like it's worth my time now to see it again.
An all time classic. Please dont see the sequels. Itll only ruin the legacy
